title: Historical - Export controls on Computers have been around a long time
gist: A forum post uses a 1999 example, Apple lobbying to lift a supercomputer-classification export ban on the 500 MHz PowerPC G4 that barred sales to over 50 nations, to argue that today's AI/GPU export controls are a continuation of a decades-old pattern rather than a novel phenomenon.

insights.claim: Current controls on exporting AI chips and GPUs are not new: in 1999 the US classified Apple's 500 MHz PowerPC G4 as a supercomputer and prohibited its export to over 50 nations.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Historical framing suggests performance-threshold export controls are a cyclical, enduring feature of US tech policy, tempering the view that today's GPU curbs are unprecedented.
insights.quote: The Power Mac G4 is so fast that it is classified as a supercomputer by the U.S. government, and we are prohibited from exporting it to over 50 nations worldwide

insights.claim: The poster implicitly notes how quickly performance thresholds become obsolete, wryly questioning what would happen if 500 MHz processors fell into adversaries' hands, a benchmark trivial by modern standards.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Illustrates that hardware export thresholds age fast, implying today's AI-chip limits may look similarly quaint within years, a caution on the durability of control regimes.
insights.quote: who knows what would happen if 500 MHz processors fell into the hands of our adversaries??
